Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

## GENERALIZATION IN DIFFUSION MODELS ARISES FROM - GEOMETRY ADAPTIVE HARMONIC REPRESENTATIONS

## **Zahra Kadkhodaie**

Ctr. for Data Science, New York University zk388@nyu.edu

## **Florentin Guth**
Ctr. for Data Science, New York University Flatiron Institute, Simons Foundation florentin.guth@nyu.edu

## **Eero P. Simoncelli**

New York University Flatiron Institute, Simons Foundation eero.simoncelli@nyu.edu

## **Stéphane Mallat**

Collège de France Flatiron Institute, Simons Foundation stephane.mallat@ens.fr

## ABSTRACT

Deep neural networks (DNNs) trained for image denoising are able to generate highquality samples with score-based reverse diffusion algorithms. These impressive capabilities seem to imply an escape from the curse of dimensionality, but recent reports of memorization of the training set raise the question of whether these networks are learning the “true” continuous density of the data. Here, we show that two DNNs trained on non-overlapping subsets of a dataset learn nearly the same score function, and thus the same density, when the number of training images is large enough. In this regime of strong generalization, diffusion-generated images are distinct from the training set, and are of high visual quality, suggesting that the inductive biases of the DNNs are well-aligned with the data density. We analyze the learned denoising functions and show that the inductive biases give rise to a shrinkage operation in a basis adapted to the underlying image. Examination of these bases reveals oscillating harmonic structures along contours and in homogeneous regions. We demonstrate that trained denoisers are inductively biased towards these geometry-adaptive harmonic bases since they arise not only when the network is trained on photographic images, but also when it is trained on image classes supported on low-dimensional manifolds for which the harmonic basis is suboptimal. Finally, we show that when trained on regular image classes for which the optimal basis is known to be geometry-adaptive and harmonic, the denoising performance of the networks is near-optimal.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep neural networks (DNNs) have demonstrated ever-more impressive capabilities for sampling from high-dimensional image densities, most recently through the development of diffusion methods. These methods operate by training a denoiser, which provides an estimate of the score (the gradient of the log of the noisy image distribution). The score is then used to sample from the corresponding estimated density, using an iterative reverse diffusion procedure (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Song & Ermon, 2019; Ho et al., 2020; Kadkhodaie & Simoncelli, 2020). However, approximating a continuous density in a high-dimensional space is notoriously difficult: do these networks actually achieve this feat, learning from a relatively small training set to generate high-quality samples, in apparent defiance of the curse of dimensionality? If so, this must be due to their inductive biases, that is, the restrictions that the architecture and optimization place on the learned denoising function. But the approximation class associated with these models is not well understood. Here, we take several steps toward elucidating this mystery.

Several recently reported results show that, when the training set is small relative to the network capacity, diffusion generative models do not approximate a continuous density, but rather memorize samples of the training set, which are then reproduced (or recombined) when generating new samples

(Somepalli et al., 2023; Carlini et al., 2023). This is a form of overfitting (high model variance). Here, we confirm this behavior for DNNs trained on small data sets, but demonstrate that these same models do not memorize when trained on sufficiently large sets. Specifically, we show that two denoisers trained on sufficiently large non-overlapping sets converge to essentially the same denoising function. That is, the learned model becomes independent of the training set (i.e., model variance falls to zero). As a result, when used for image generation, these networks produce nearly identical samples. These results provide stronger and more direct evidence of generalization than standard comparisons of average performance on train and test sets. This generalization can be achieved with large but realizable training sets (for our examples, roughly 10[5] images suffices), reflecting powerful inductive biases of these networks. Moreover, sampling from these models produces images of high visual quality, implying that these inductive biases are well-matched to the underlying distribution of photographic images (Wilson & Izmailov, 2020; Goyal & Bengio, 2022; Griffiths et al., 2023).

To study these inductive biases, we develop and exploit the relationship between denoising and density estimation. We find that DNN denoisers trained on photographic images perform a shrinkage operation in an orthonormal basis consisting of harmonic functions that are adapted to the geometry of features in the underlying image. We refer to these as _geometry-adaptive harmonic bases_ (GAHBs). This observation, taken together with the generalization performance of DNN denoisers, suggests that optimal bases for denoising photographic images are GAHBs and, moreover, that inductive biases of DNN denoisers encourage such bases. To test this more directly, we examine a particular class of images whose intensity variations are regular over regions separated by regular contours. A particular type of GAHB, known as “bandlets” (Peyré & Mallat, 2008), have been shown to be near-optimal for denoising these images (Dossal et al., 2011). We observe that the DNN denoiser operates within a GAHB similar to a bandlet basis, also achieving near-optimal performance. Thus the inductive bias enables the network to appropriately estimate the score in these cases.

If DNN denoisers induce biases towards the GAHB approximation class, then they should perform sub-optimally for distributions whose optimal bases are not GAHBs. To investigate this, we train DNN denoisers on image classes supported on low-dimensional manifolds, for which the optimal denoising basis is only partially constrained. Specifically, an optimal denoiser (for small noise) should project a noisy image on the tangent space of the manifold. We observe that the DNN denoiser closely approximates this projection, but also partially retains content lying within a subspace spanned by a set of additional GAHB vectors. These suboptimal components reflect the GAHB inductive bias.

## 2 DIFFUSION MODEL VARIANCE AND DENOISING GENERALIZATION

Consider an unknown image probability density, _p_ ( _x_ ). Rather than approximating this density directly, diffusion models learn the scores of the distributions of noise-corrupted images. Here, we show that the denoising error provides a bound on the density modeling error, and use this to analyze the convergence of the density model.

## 2.1 DIFFUSION MODELS AND DENOISING

Let _y_ = _x_ + _z_ where _z ∼N_ (0 _, σ_[2] Id). The density _pσ_ ( _y_ ) of noisy images is then related to _p_ ( _x_ ) through marginalization over _x_ :

where _gσ_ ( _z_ ) is the density of _z_ . Hence, _pσ_ ( _y_ ) is obtained by convolving _p_ ( _x_ ) with a Gaussian with standard deviation _σ_ . The family of densities _{pσ_ ( _y_ ); _σ ≥_ 0 _}_ forms a scale-space representation of _p_ ( _x_ ), analogous to the temporal evolution of a diffusion process.

Diffusion models learn an approximation _sθ_ ( _y_ ) (dropping the _σ_ dependence for simplicity) of the scores _∇_ log _pσ_ ( _y_ ) of the blurred densities _pσ_ ( _y_ ) at all noise levels _σ_ . The collection of these score models implicitly defines a model _pθ_ ( _x_ ) of the density of clean images _p_ ( _x_ ) through a reverse diffusion process. The error of the generative model, as measured by the KL divergence between _p_ ( _x_ ) and _pθ_ ( _x_ ), is then controlled by the integrated score error across all noise levels (Song et al., 2021):

so that _fθ_ ( _y_ ) _≈_ E _x_ [ _x | y_ ]. This estimated conditional mean is used to recover the estimated score using eq. (3): _sθ_ ( _y_ ) = ( _fθ_ ( _y_ ) _− y_ ) _/σ_[2] . As we show in Appendix D.2, the error in estimating the density _p_ ( _x_ ) is bounded by the integrated optimality gap of the denoiser across noise levels:

where _f[⋆]_ ( _y_ ) = E _x_ [ _x | y_ ] is the optimal denoiser. Thus, learning the true density model is equivalent to performing optimal denoising at all noise levels. Conversely, a suboptimal denoiser introduces a score approximation error, which in turn can result in an error in the modeled density.

Generally, the optimal denoising function _f[⋆]_ (as well as the “true” distribution, _p_ ( _x_ )) is unknown for photographic images, which makes numerical evaluation of sub-optimality challenging. We can however separate deviations from optimality arising from model bias and model variance. Model variance measures the size of the approximation class, and hence the strength (or restrictiveness) of the inductive biases. It can be evaluated without knowledge of _f[⋆]_ . Here, we define generalization as near-zero model variance (i.e., an absence of overfitting), which is agnostic to model bias. This is the subject of Section 2.2. Model bias measures the distance of the true score to the approximation class, and thus the alignment between the inductive biases and the data distribution. In the context of photographic images, visual quality of generated samples can be a qualitative indicator of the model bias, although high visual quality does not necessarily guarantee low model bias. We evaluate model bias in Section 3.2 by considering synthetic image classes for which _f[⋆]_ is approximately known.

## 2.2 TRANSITION FROM MEMORIZATION TO GENERALIZATION

DNNs are susceptible to overfitting, because the number of training examples is typically small relative to the model capacity. Since density estimation, in particular, suffers from the curse of dimensionality, overfitting is of more concern in the context of generative models. An overfitted denoiser performs well on training images but fails to generalize to test images, resulting in lowdiversity generated images. Consistent with this, several papers have reported that diffusion models can memorize their training data (Somepalli et al., 2023; Carlini et al., 2023; Dar et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). To directly assess this, we compared denoising performance on training and test data for different training set sizes _N_ . We trained denoisers on subsets of the (downsampled) CelebA dataset (Liu et al., 2015) of size _N_ = 10[0] _,_ 10[1] _,_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 10[4] _,_ 10[5] . We used a UNet architecture (Ronneberger et al., 2015), which is composed of 3 convolutional encoder and decoder blocks with rectifying non-linearities. These denoisers are universal and blind: they operate on all noise levels without having noise level as an input Mohan* et al. (2020). Networks are trained to minimize mean squared error (4). See Appendix A for architecture and training details.

Results are shown in Figure 1. When _N_ = 1, the denoiser essentially memorizes the single training image, leading to a high test error. Increasing _N_ substantially increases the performance on the test set while worsening performance on the training set, as the network transitions from memorization to generalization. At _N_ = 10[5] , empirical test and train error are matched for all noise levels.

To investigate this generalization further, we train denoisers on _non-overlapping_ subsets of CelebA of various size _N_ . We then generate samples using the scores learned by each denoiser, through the reverse diffusion algorithm of Kadkhodaie & Simoncelli (2020)—see Appendix A for details. Figure 2 shows samples generated by these denoisers, initialized from the same noise sample. For small _N_ , the networks memorize their respective training images. However, for large _N_ , the networks converge to the same score function (and thus sample from the same model density), generating nearly identical samples. This surprising behavior provides a much stronger demonstration of convergence than comparison of average train and test performance.

Each curve shows the denoising error (output PSNR, ten times log10 ratio of squared dynamic range to MSE) as a function of noise level (input PSNR), for a training set of size _N_ . As _N_ increases, performance on the training set generally worsens (left), while performance on the test set improves (right). For _N_ = 1 and _N_ = 10, the train PSNR improves with unit slope, while test PSNR is poor, independent of noise level, a sign of memorization. The increase in test performance on small noise levels at _N_ = 1000 is indicative of the transition phase from memorization to generalization. At _N_ = 10[5] , test and train PSNR are essentially identical, and the model is no longer overfitting the training data.

## 3 INDUCTIVE BIASES

The number of samples needed for estimation of an arbitrary probability density grows exponentially with dimensionality (the “curse of dimensionality”). As a result, estimating high-dimensional distributions is only feasible if one imposes strong constraints or priors over the hypothesis space. In a diffusion model, these arise from the network architecture and the optimization algorithm, and are referred to as the inductive biases of the network (Wilson & Izmailov, 2020; Goyal & Bengio, 2022; Griffiths et al., 2023). In Section 2.2, we demonstrated that DNN denoisers can learn scores (and thus a density) from relatively small training sets. This generalization result, combined with the high quality of sampled images, is evidence that the inductive biases are well-matched to the “true” distribution of images, allowing the model to rapidly converge to a good solution through learning. On the contrary, when inductive biases are not aligned with the true distribution, the model will arrive at a poor solution with high model bias.

For diffusion methods, learning the right density model is equivalent to performing optimal denoising at all noise levels (see Section 2.1). The inductive biases on the density model thus arise directly from inductive biases in the denoiser. This connection offers a means of evaluating the accuracy of the learned probability models, which is generally difficult in high-dimensions.

## 3.1 DENOISING AS SHRINKAGE IN AN ADAPTIVE BASIS

The inductive biases of the DNN denoiser can be studied through an eigendecomposition of its Jacobian. We describe the general properties that are expected for an optimal denoiser, and examine several specific cases for which the optimal solution is partially known.

**Jacobian eigenvectors as an adaptive basis.** To analyze inductive biases, we perform a local ˆ analysis of a denoising estimator _x_ ( _y_ ) = _f_ ( _y_ ) by looking at its Jacobian _∇f_ ( _y_ ). For simplicity, we assume that the Jacobian is symmetric and non-negative (we show below that this holds for the optimal denoiser, and it is approximately true of the network Jacobian (Mohan* et al., 2020)). We can then diagonalize it to obtain eigenvalues ( _λk_ ( _y_ ))1 _≤k≤d_ and eigenvectors ( _ek_ ( _y_ ))1 _≤k≤d_ .

If _f_ ( _y_ ) is computed with a DNN denoiser with no additive “bias” parameters, its input-output mapping is piecewise linear, as opposed to piecewise affine (Mohan* et al., 2020; Romano et al., 2017). It

**Best adaptive bases.** Adapting the basis ( _ek_ )1 _≤k≤d_ to the noisy image _y_ allows obtaining sparser representations of the unknown clean image _x_ with a faster decay, and thus a larger PSNR slope. To calculate the optimal adaptive basis, we need to find an oracle denoiser that has the same asymptotic MSE as a non-oracle denoiser, yielding matching lower and upper bounds on the asymptotic MSE.

Consider an oracle denoiser which performs a thresholding in an oracle basis ( _ek_ ( _x_ )) that depends on the unknown clean image _x_ . The above analysis then still applies, and if the coefficients _⟨x, ek_ ( _x_ ) _⟩_[2] decay as _k[−]_[(] _[α]_[+1)] , then the asymptotic PSNR slope is again _α/_ ( _α_ + 1). The best oracle basis satisfies _e_ 1( _x_ ) = _x/∥x∥_ , but it yields a loose lower bound as it cannot be estimated from the noisy image _y_ alone. We thus restrict the oracle denoiser to choose the basis ( _ek_ ( _x_ )) within a fixed dictionary. A larger dictionary increases adaptivity, but it then becomes harder to estimate the basis that best represents _x_ from _y_ alone. If the dictionary of bases is constructed from a number of vectors _ek_ which is polynomial in the dimension _d_ (the number of bases can however be exponential in _d_ ) then a thresholding in the basis ( _ek_ ( _y_ )) that best approximates the noisy image _y_ achieves the same slope as the oracle denoiser (Barron et al., 1999; Dossal et al., 2011). This near-optimality despite the presence of noise comes from the limited choice of possible basis vectors _ek_ in the dictionary, which limits the variance of the best-basis estimation, e.g. by preventing _e_ 1( _y_ ) = _y/∥y∥_ . The main difficulty is then to design a small-enough dictionary that gives optimal representations of images from the data distribution in order to achieve the optimal PSNR slope.

We now evaluate the inductive biases of DNN denoisers through this lens. In Section 2, we showed that the DNN denoisers overcome the curse of dimensionality: their variance decays to zero in the generalization regime. In the next section, we explain this observation by demonstrating that they are inductively biased towards adaptive bases _ek_ ( _y_ ) from a particular class.

## 3.2 GEOMETRY-ADAPTIVE HARMONIC BASES IN DNNS

Figure 3 shows the shrinkage factors ( _λk_ ( _y_ )), adaptive basis vectors ( _ek_ ( _y_ )), and signal coefficients ( _⟨x, ek_ ( _y_ ) _⟩_ ) of a DNN denoiser trained on 10[5] face images. The eigenvectors have oscillating patterns both along the contours and in uniformly regular regions and thus adapt to the geometry of the input image. We call this a geometry-adaptive harmonic basis (GAHB). The coefficients are sparse in this basis, and the fast rate of decay of eigenvalues exploits this sparsity. The high quality of generated images and the strong generalization results of Section 2 show that DNN denoisers rely on inductive biases that are well-aligned to photographic image distributions. All of this suggests that DNN denoisers might be inductively biased towards GAHBs. In the following, we provide evidence supporting this conjecture by analyzing networks trained on synthetic datasets where the optimal solution is (approximately) known.

**C** _[α]_ **images and bandlet bases.** If DNNs are inductively biased towards GAHBs, we expect that they generalize and converge to the optimal denoising performance when such bases are optimal. We

Figure 4: UNet denoisers trained on 10[5] **C** _[α]_ images achieve near-optimal performance. **Left.** PSNR curves for various regularity levels _α_ . The empirical slopes closely match the theoretical optimal slopes (parenthesized values, dashed lines). **Right.** A **C** _[α]_ image ( _α_ = 4) of size 80 _×_ 80 and its top eigenvectors, which consist of harmonics on the two regions and harmonics along the boundary. The frequency of the harmonics increases with _k_ . More examples are given in Appendix C.1.

consider the so-called geometric **C** _[α]_ class of images (Korostelev & Tsybakov, 1993; Donoho, 1999; Peyré & Mallat, 2008) which consist of regular contours on regular backgrounds, where the degree of regularity is controlled by _α_ . Examples of these images are shown in Figure 4 and Appendix C.1. A mathematical definition and an algorithm for their synthesis are presented in Appendix E.

Optimal sparse representations of **C** _[α]_ images are obtained with “bandlet” bases (Peyré & Mallat, 2008). Bandlets are harmonic functions oscillating at different frequencies, whose geometry is adapted to the directional regularity of images along contours. Geometric **C** _[α]_ images can be represented with few bandlets having low-frequency oscillations in regular regions and along contours but sharp variations across contours. The _k_ -th coefficient in the best bandlet basis then decays as _k[−]_[(] _[α]_[+1)] . It follows that the optimal denoiser has a PSNR which asymptotically increases with a slope _α/_ ( _α_ + 1) as a function of input PSNR (Korostelev & Tsybakov, 1993; Dossal et al., 2011).

Figure 4 shows that DNN denoisers trained on **C** _[α]_ images also achieve this optimal rate and learns GAHBs, similarly to bandlets but with a more flexible geometry. This generalization performance confirms that inductive biases of DNNs favor GAHBs.

**Low-dimensional manifolds.** If DNNs are inductively biased towards GAHBs, then we expect these bases to emerge even in cases where they are suboptimal. To test this prediction, we consider a dataset of disk images with varying positions, sizes, and foreground/background intensities. This defines a five-dimensional _curved_ manifold, with a tangent space evaluated at a disk image _x_ that is spanned by deformations of _x_ along these five dimensions. When the noise level _σ_ is much smaller than the radius of curvature of the manifold, the posterior distribution _p_ ( _x|y_ ) is supported on an approximately flat region of the manifold, and the optimal denoiser is approximately a projection onto the tangent space. Thus, the optimal Jacobian should have only five non-negligible eigenvalues, whose corresponding eigenvectors span the tangent space. The remaining eigenvectors should have shrinkage factors of _λ_ = 0, but are otherwise unconstrained. The optimal MSE is asymptotically equal to 5 _σ_[2] , corresponding to a PSNR slope of one.

Figure 5 shows an analysis of a denoiser trained on 10[5] disk images, of size 80 _×_ 80. We observe additional basis vectors with non-negligible eigenvalues that have a GAHB structure, with oscillations on the background region and along the contour of the disk. We also find that the number of non-zero eigenvalues _increases_ as the noise level decreases, leading to a suboptimal PSNR slope that is less than 1 _._ 0. These results reveal that the inductive biases of the DNN are not perfectly aligned with low-dimensional manifolds, and that in the presence of the curvature, this suboptimality increases as the noise level decreases. We obtain similar results on two additional examples of a distribution supported on a low-dimensional manifold, given in Appendix C.2.

**Shuffled faces.** We also consider in Appendix C.3 a dataset of shuffled faces, obtained by applying a common permutation to the pixels of each face image. This permutation does not preserve locality between neighboring pixels, and thus the optimal basis does not have harmonic structure. The resulting mismatch between the DNN inductive biases and the data distribution result in substantially worse performance than for the original (unscrambled) faces.

The first five basis vectors closely match the basis of the tangent space of the manifold evaluated at the clean image. In contrast, the next five are GAHBs that lie along contours and within background regions of the clean image.

## 4 DISCUSSION

Diffusion generative models, which operate through iterative application of a trained DNN denoiser, have recently surpassed all previous methods of learning probability models of images. Their training objective (minimization of squared denoising error) is simple and robust, and they generate samples of impressive quality. In this paper, we elucidate the approximation properties that underlie this success, by analyzing the trained denoiser, which is directly related to the score function, and to the density from which the samples are drawn.

We show empirically that diffusion models memorize samples when trained on small sets, but transition to a strong form of generalization as the training set size increases, converging to a unique density model that is independent of the specific training samples. The amount of data needed to reach this phase transition is very small relative to the size of dataset needed for convergence without any inductive biases, and depends on the image size and complexity relative to the neural network capacity (Yoon et al., 2023). It is of interest to extend both the theory and the empirical studies to account for the interplay of these factors. Appendix B.4 shows preliminary results in this direction.

We also examined the inductive biases that enable this strong generalization. Using a well-established mathematical framework, we showed that DNN denoisers perform shrinkage of noisy coefficients in a geometry-adaptive harmonic basis (GAHB) which is shaped by geometric features of the image. For the **C** _[α]_ class of images, such geometric bases are known to be optimal, and DNN denoisers achieve near-optimal performance on this class. Previous mathematical literature has shown that bandlet bases, which are a specific type of GAHB, are near-optimal for this class, but the GAHBs learned by the DNN denoiser are more general and more flexible. For images drawn from low-dimensional manifolds, for which the optimal basis spans the tangent subspace of the manifold, we find that DNN denoisers achieve good denoising within a basis aligned with this subspace, but also incorporate GAHB vectors in the remaining unconstrained dimensions. The non-suppressed noise along these additional GAHB components leads to suboptimal denoising performance. This observation, along with similar ones shown in Appendix C.2, provide more supporting evidence for the hypothesis that inductive biases of DNN denoisers promote GAHBs.

We do not provide a formal mathematical definition of the class of GAHBs arising from the inductive biases of DNNs. Convolutions in DNN architectures, whose eigenvectors are sinusoids, presumably engender GAHB harmonic structure, but the geometric adaptivity must arise from interactions with rectification nonlinearities (ReLUs). A more precise elucidation of this GAHB function class, and its role in shaping inductive biases of the DNNs used in a wide variety of other tasks and modalities, is of fundamental interest.

## A EXPERIMENTAL DETAILS

## A.1 TRAINING AND ARCHITECTURE DETAILS

**Architectures.** We performed empirical experiments using two different architectures: UNet, and BF-CNN. All the denoisers are “bias-free”: we remove all additive constants from convolution and batch-normalization operations (i.e., the batch normalization does not subtract the mean). This facilitates unversality (denoisers can operate at all noise levels), and interpretability (network transformations are homogeneous of order 1, and the Jacobian provides a local characterization) - see Mohan* et al. (2020).

UNet networks contain 3 decoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks (Ronneberger et al., 2015). Each block consists of 2 convolutional layers followed by a ReLU non-linearity and bias-free batch-normalization. Each encoder block is followed by a 2 _×_ 2 spacial down-sampling and a 2 fold increase in the number of channels. Each decoder block is followed by a 2 _×_ 2 spacial upsampling and a 2 fold reduction of channels. The total number of parameters is 7 _._ 6 _m_ .

BF-CNN networks Mohan* et al. (2020) are bias-free versions of DNCNN networks (Zhang et al., 2017), contain 21 convolutional layers with no subsampling, each consisting of 64 channels. Each layer, except for the first and the last, is followed by a ReLU non-linearity and bias-free batchnormalization. All convolutional kernels are of size 3 _×_ 3, resulting in 700 _k_ parameters in total.

**Training.** We follow the training procedure described in Mohan* et al. (2020), minimizing the mean squared error in denoising images corrupted by i.i.d. Gaussian noise with standard deviations drawn from the range [0 _,_ 1] (relative to image intensity range [0 _,_ 1]). Training is carried out on batches of size 512, for 1000 epochs. Note that all denoisers are universal and blind: they are trained to handle a range of noise, and the noise level is not provided as input to the denoiser. These properties are exploited by the sampling algorithm, which can operate without manual specification of the step size schedule (Kadkhodaie & Simoncelli, 2020). This method produces high-quality results in generative sampling, as well as sampling conditioned on linear measurements (Kadkhodaie & Simoncelli, 2021).

**Datasets.** For experiments shown in Figures 1 to 3 and 7, we use the CelebA dataset (Liu et al., 2015) downsampled to 80 _×_ 80 resolution. For experiments shown in Figures 9 and 10, we use images drawn from the LSUN bedroom dataset (Yu et al., 2015) downsampled to 80 _×_ 80 resolution. This dataset is downsampled to 32 _×_ 32 resolution for experiments shown in Figure 13. For experiments shown in Figure 11 we use CelebA HQ dataset (Karras et al., 2018) downsampled to 40 _×_ 40 resolution.

We chose _h_ = 0 _._ 01, _β_ = 0 _._ 1, _σ_ 0 = 1, and _σ∞_ = 0 _._ 05.

This paper addresses a fundamental question in generative modeling: how do diffusion models escape the curse of dimensionality to generalize rather than simply memorize training data? The authors provide a compelling, two-pronged argument using empirical evidence of model convergence and a theoretical analysis of "geometry-adaptive harmonic bases" (GAHBs). The quality of the scholarship is high, evidenced by the rigorous definition of generalization—demonstrating that two networks trained on non-overlapping sets converge to the same score function—which is a much stronger proof of generalization than standard train/test loss comparisons. The clarity is excellent; the transition from the concept of inductive bias to the specific identification of GAHB-like structures (reminiscent of bandlets) provides a rare bridge between modern deep learning and classical signal processing theory. 

The most significant issues or areas for potential refinement concern the scope of the "GAHB" claim. While the authors successfully link the network’s inductive bias to harmonic structures, the transition from "observed oscillations" to a "general theory of generalization" requires careful handling. Specifically, it remains to be seen if this bias is a result of the convolutional architecture (U-Net) commonly used in diffusion or an inherent property of SGD-based optimization on image manifolds. Furthermore, while the paper highlights a "near-optimal" performance on specific regular image classes, a more critical discussion on where this inductive bias fails (beyond the brief mention of low-dimensional manifolds) would strengthen the "sub-optimality" argument. Overall, this is a sophisticated paper that moves the discourse from "whether" diffusion models generalize to "how" they do so through specific structural representations.

comments.title: Missing Equations for Denoiser Mapping and SURE
comments.quote:
it follows that the denoiser mapping can be rewritten in terms of the Jacobian eigendecomposition as

[Equation 6 placeholder]

The denoiser can thus be interpreted as performing shrinkage...
comments.explanation: In the transition from the discussion of piecewise linearity to Equation 6 (which is missing in the OCR/text), the text claims the denoiser performs 'shrinkage' with factors $\lambda_k(y)$. For a piecewise linear function $f(y) = \nabla f(y) y$, the expression should be $f(y) = \sum_k \lambda_k(y) \langle y, e_k(y) \rangle e_k(y)$. While the prose correctly describes the intuition of shrinkage, the actual mathematical form is not explicitly provided in the passage. Similarly, the expression for SURE (Equation 7) is referenced but the formula itself is absent, which is necessary to understand the subsequent derivation involving the 'rank' of the Jacobian and the trace. While I can infer the authors intended $\text{SURE}(f) = \|y - f(y)\|^2 - d\sigma^2 + 2\sigma^2 \text{div}(f(y))$, the text currently jumps from the mention of the estimate to the trade-off logic without the linking math.

comments.title: Incomplete and Misplaced Equation for Optimal Jacobian
comments.quote:
It is shown in Appendix D.1 that the optimal minimum MSE denoiser and its Jacobian are given by

[Equation 8 placeholder]

That is, the Jacobian of the optimal denoiser is proportional to the posterior covariance matrix
comments.explanation: The text references Equation 8 to define the optimal denoiser and its Jacobian. Based on the summary provided earlier, the optimal denoiser is $f^*(y) = \mathbb{E}[x|y]$. The Jacobian of this estimator is known to be $\nabla f^*(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \text{Cov}(x|y)$. The passage correctly identifies it as 'proportional to the posterior covariance matrix,' but the equation is missing. Furthermore, the text below Equation 9 mentions 'the first equality' of a formula that is not fully rendered. A reader needs the explicit link $\nabla f^*(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \Sigma_{x|y}$ to follow the argument that small eigenvalues correspond to low-rank approximations of the data manifold.

comments.title: Inconsistent Thresholding Coefficients in Oracle MSE
comments.quote: which is minimized when $\lambda_k ( x ) = \langle x,e_k \rangle [2] + \sigma [2] [.] [ The coefficient] [ \lambda][k] [(] [x] [)][ thus acts as a soft threshold:]
comments.explanation: There appears to be an OCR or notation error in the expression for the optimal oracle shrinkage factor. For an oracle MSE minimizer $\lambda_k$, the factor should be Wiener-like: $\lambda_k = \frac{\langle x, e_k \rangle^2}{\langle x, e_k \rangle^2 + \sigma^2}$. The current text reads as a sum or a fragment '$\langle x,e_k \rangle^2 + \sigma^2$', and the subsequent prose refers to it as a 'soft threshold.' In signal processing, soft-thresholding usually refers to the mapping $\eta(w) = \text{sgn}(w)(|w|-	au)_+$, whereas the oracle weights in a fixed basis are typically the aforementioned ratio. If the authors meant the 'hard thresholding' approximation where $\lambda_k$ is 1 or 0, the text is ambiguous as to whether $\lambda_k$ is a weight or the resulting coefficient.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in optimal denoising performance for shuffled data
comments.quote: By construction, the optimal denoiser on shuffled faces has the same performance as the optimal denoiser on ordinary faces (unshuffling the image pixels, optimally denoising the face image, and then shuffling the pixels back).
comments.explanation: The text states that the 'optimal' denoiser for shuffled faces performs identically to the 'optimal' denoiser for ordinary faces because a permutation is a unitary operator that preserves the $L^2$ norm (and thus MSE). While this is mathematically true for an *unconstrained* optimal estimator (like the true posterior mean), the paper's central thesis is that DNNs have an inductive bias toward 'Geometry-Adaptive Harmonic Bases' (GAHB). Earlier, the authors claim that 'shuffling pixels significantly degrades DNN performance.' If the 'optimal' performance is identical, but the DNN's performance is 'significantly worse' (as stated later in the same paragraph), it implies the DNN is no longer reaching or approximating that optimum. However, Figure 4 and the Section 3.1 discussion define 'near-optimality' in terms of the ability to achieve a specific PSNR slope. A reader might be confused as to whether 'optimal' here refers to the theoretical Bayes risk (which is invariant to shuffling) or the 'near-optimal' performance of a sparse estimator within a specific dictionary. If the dictionary is fixed to GAHBs (as the inductive bias suggests), then once the pixels are shuffled, the GAHB dictionary is no longer aligned with the signal, and 'optimal' denoising *within that class* would actually be much worse. The sentence could be clarified to distinguish between the invariant Bayes risk and the architecture-constrained performance.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reported shrinkage factor behavior
comments.quote: The shrinkage factors λk ( y ) decay more slowly than when the denoiser is trained on non-shuffled faces (Figure 3), which is indicative of suboptimality
comments.explanation: Earlier in Section 3.1 and Figure 3, the paper establishes that a *rapid* decay of coefficients/eigenvalues indicates that image content is concentrated in a small subspace, which leads to better denoising (a higher PSNR slope). In Figure 5, the authors attribute suboptimality on manifolds to the presence of 'additional basis vectors with non-negligible eigenvalues' (i.e., slow decay) that capture noise. In the case of shuffled faces, the text says a slower decay of $\lambda_k$ is indicative of suboptimality. This is consistent with the theory that the model is failing to 'zero out' the dimensions containing only noise because the signal is no longer sparse in the model's preferred basis. However, if the basis vectors (when unshuffled) still look like 'GAHBs adapted to the geometry of the face' as claimed at the end of the passage, it is hard to reconcile why the shrinkage factors $\lambda_k$ (which determine the filter's aggressiveness) would decay *slower* unless the network is struggling to distinguish signal from noise in the scrambled domain. Using 'slower decay' to mean 'worse' requires the reader to assume the excess 'tail' of the eigenvalues represents noise leakage, which is plausible but could use a more explicit link to the 'noise suppression' argument used in the manifold section.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a central and timely question in generative modeling—namely the distinction between true generalization and memorization in diffusion models. The framing is excellent, moving beyond standard train/test metrics to a more sophisticated analysis of model variance and the convergence of learned score functions. The central hypothesis, that generalization arises from an inductive bias towards geometry-adaptive harmonic representations (GAHBs), is both specific and intellectually compelling, connecting deep learning performance to fundamental concepts in harmonic analysis. The paper's ultimate success, however, rests on two critical challenges that the full manuscript must convincingly address. First, the empirical claim that independently trained denoisers converge to the "same" function requires a rigorous and quantitative definition of similarity, which will be crucial for substantiating the generalization argument. More significantly, the paper's primary contribution hinges on the ability to concretely define, identify, and analyze these "geometry-adaptive harmonic bases" within the weights or activations of a trained DNN. The introduction promises this analysis, but the methodology for extracting such a structured basis from a black-box network remains the most significant unanswered question and will be the primary determinant of the paper's impact.

comments.quote:
Further, the optimal denoising error is then given by (see Appendix D.1 for the first equality)
$\mathbb{E}[\|f^*(y) - x\|^2] = \mathbb{E}[\text{tr}(\text{Cov}(x|y))] = d\sigma^2 - \mathbb{E}[\text{tr}(\nabla f^*(y))]$
comments.explanation:
The paper states that for the optimal denoiser, $
abla f^*(y) =  rac{	ext{Cov}(x|y)}{\sigma^2}$. Taking the trace of both sides gives $	ext{tr}(
abla f^*(y)) =  rac{	ext{tr}(	ext{Cov}(x|y))}{\sigma^2}$. This implies $	ext{tr}(	ext{Cov}(x|y)) = \sigma^2 	ext{tr}(
abla f^*(y))$. However, the paper claims $\mathbb{E}[	ext{tr}(	ext{Cov}(x|y))] = d\sigma^2 - \mathbb{E}[	ext{tr}(
abla f^*(y))]$, which is inconsistent with the direct relationship derived from the Jacobian expression. This discrepancy could be due to (1) a different formulation in Appendix D.1, (2) a typo in the equation, or (3) some additional context that modifies the relationship. Without access to Appendix D.1, this appears to be a technical inconsistency that needs clarification.

comments.title: Missing oracle denoising error formula
comments.quote:
Appendix D.4 shows that the denoising error of this oracle is

_⟨x,ek⟩_[2] which is minimized when _λk_ ( _x_ ) = _⟨x,ek⟩_[2] + _σ_[2] _[.]_[
comments.explanation: The text appears to be missing the actual formula for the oracle denoising error. After "Appendix D.4 shows that the denoising error of this oracle is", there's a line break and then just "_⟨x,ek⟩_[2]" which seems incomplete. Based on the context and the paper's summary, this should be the formula for the oracle denoising error: E[∥x - f_oracle(x)∥^2] = E[Σ_k (σ^2 ⟨x, e_k⟩^2) / (⟨x, e_k⟩^2 + σ^2)]. The subsequent text correctly identifies the minimizing λ_k(x), but the error formula itself is missing, making the argument harder to follow. This appears to be an OCR error where the formula wasn't properly extracted.

comments.title: Insufficient explanation for counterintuitive eigenvalue behavior with decreasing noise
comments.quote: We also find that the number of non-zero eigenvalues _increases_ as the noise level decreases, leading to a suboptimal PSNR slope that is less than 1 _._ 0. These results reveal that the inductive biases of the DNN are not perfectly aligned with low-dimensional manifolds, and that in the presence of the curvature, this suboptimality increases as the noise level decreases.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that as noise decreases, the DNN denoiser deviates more from the optimal solution (which should have exactly 5 non-zero eigenvalues for a 5D manifold). This counterintuitive behavior - where the network becomes less optimal with less noise - is not adequately explained. One would expect that with less noise, it would be easier to identify the correct tangent space. The authors attribute this to 'the presence of curvature' but don't explain why curvature effects would worsen with decreasing noise or how curvature is measured for this manifold. This appears to be a key empirical observation that supports their main claim about GAHB bias, but the mechanism is unclear.

comments.title: Underspecified disk dataset parameters
comments.quote: We consider a dataset of disk images with varying positions, sizes, and foreground/background intensities.
comments.explanation: The description of the disk manifold dataset lacks crucial details for reproducibility. The ranges and distributions for positions, sizes, and intensities are not specified. For example: What is the range of disk radii? Are positions uniformly distributed across the image? What are the intensity ranges? These parameters affect the manifold's curvature and could influence the observed behavior. Without these details, readers cannot assess whether the results might be artifacts of particular parameter choices.

comments.title: Missing justification for optimal eigenvalue structure
comments.quote: Thus, the optimal Jacobian should have only five non-negligible eigenvalues, whose corresponding eigenvectors span the tangent space. The remaining eigenvectors should have shrinkage factors of _λ_ = 0, but are otherwise unconstrained.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the optimal solution should have exactly 5 non-zero eigenvalues corresponding to the tangent space dimensions, with all others having λ = 0. While this is intuitive for projection onto a subspace, it's not immediately obvious why the remaining eigenvectors are completely unconstrained rather than being driven to λ = 0 through the optimization. A brief explanation of why this structure is optimal would help readers understand the baseline against which the DNN's behavior is being compared.

comments.title: Incorrect distinction between piecewise linear and piecewise affine
comments.quote: If _f_ ( _y_ ) is computed with a DNN denoiser with no additive "bias" parameters, its input-output mapping is piecewise linear, as opposed to piecewise affine (Mohan* et al., 2020; Romano et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The authors make an incorrect distinction between 'piecewise linear' and 'piecewise affine'. A function is piecewise linear if it's linear on each piece of its domain, while a function is piecewise affine if it's affine (linear plus a constant) on each piece. A piecewise linear function is actually a special case of a piecewise affine function where the constant term is zero. The authors likely mean that bias-free networks are homogeneous of degree 1 (i.e., f(ax) = af(x)), which is different from the standard definition of piecewise linear. This could confuse readers familiar with these mathematical terms.

comments.title: Typo in UNet architecture description
comments.quote: UNet networks contain 3 decoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks (Ronneberger et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo in the UNet architecture description. It states '3 decoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks' but should likely say '3 encoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks' to match the standard UNet architecture described in Ronneberger et al. (2015). The subsequent description mentions encoder blocks with downsampling and decoder blocks with upsampling, confirming this interpretation.

comments.title: Undefined variable in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: _**x** t ←_ _**x** t−_ 1 + _h_ _**d** t_ + _γtzt ▷_ Perform a partial denoiser step and add noise
comments.explanation: In step 9 of Algorithm 1, the update uses 'dt' which is not defined anywhere in the algorithm. Step 5 computes 'st ← f(xt-1) - xt-1' which is described as 'Compute the score from the denoiser residual'. The algorithm then uses an undefined 'dt' in the update equation instead of 'st'. This appears to be either a typographical error (where 'dt' should be 'st') or an oversight where 'dt' should be defined. Without clarification, the algorithm is ambiguous and would be difficult to reproduce correctly. Given that step 5 computes the score and step 9 mentions 'perform a partial denoiser step', it's most likely that 'dt' should be 'st', but this should be explicitly corrected or clarified.

comments.explanation: The receptive field sizes in Table 1 show a pattern of approximately halving as resolution doubles (8×18 → 4×44 → 2×92), but the last entry '88×188' breaks this pattern dramatically. Given the context and the progression of the other values, this appears to be an OCR error. The most plausible intended value would be something like '1×188' or similar to maintain the pattern. Since this is likely an OCR artifact rather than an author error, and the paper's claims don't depend on this specific value, I'm flagging this only for completeness.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for non-proportional data requirements
comments.quote: However, the number of training images does not increase proportionally with the image size: the increase in needed to hit the threshold ∆ _N_ from 80 _×_ 80 to 160 _×_ 160 images is much smaller than the ∆ _N_ from 40 _×_ 40 to 80 _×_ 80. This observation is consistent with previous reports indicating that conditioned on coarser content of the image, learning the finer details requires less data due to the conditional Markov property of images (Kadkhodaie et al., 2023).
comments.explanation: The authors make an interesting observation about the non-proportional increase in required training data as image resolution increases, attributing it to the conditional Markov property of images. While this is plausible and supported by the cited reference, the explanation is quite brief. The conditional Markov property is mentioned without any formal definition or explanation of how it specifically leads to this observed pattern. Given that this is an empirical observation that could have important implications for scaling laws, a more detailed explanation or even a brief mathematical illustration would strengthen the claim.

comments.title: Underspecified noise level sampling for PSNR gap calculation
comments.quote: Generalization is measured as the difference between train and test PSNRs averaged across noise levels _σ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1].
comments.explanation: The method for calculating the PSNR gap mentions averaging across noise levels σ ∈ [0,1], but doesn't specify how these noise levels are sampled. Are they uniformly sampled? Logarithmically spaced? How many samples? This level of detail could affect the reported values and would be important for reproducibility. While this might be specified elsewhere in the paper, it's a key methodological detail that ideally should be included here.

comments.title: Issues with shuffled faces experiment
comments.quote: By construction, the optimal denoiser on shuffled faces has the same performance as the optimal denoiser on ordinary faces (unshuffling the image pixels, optimally denoising the face image, and then shuffling the pixels back). The permutation was chosen randomly, and does not preserve locality, as neighboring pixels are mapped to independent positions.
comments.explanation: There are two issues with the shuffled faces experiment. First, the claim that optimal performance is the same for shuffled and unshuffled faces is only true if the permutation is known to the denoiser. However, the experimental setup suggests the DNN denoiser must learn to handle the shuffled pixels without explicit knowledge of the permutation. When the permutation is unknown, the denoising problem becomes fundamentally harder - the denoiser cannot exploit spatial correlations and locality that are crucial for effective denoising. Second, the passage does not clarify whether the permutation is: (1) fixed across all images in the dataset, (2) different for each image, or (3) known to the network during training. This is crucial for interpreting the results. If the permutation is fixed and known during training, the network could potentially learn to reverse it. If it varies per image or is unknown, the task becomes much harder. The experimental setup and interpretation of results depend critically on this detail.

The subsequent text correctly identifies the minimizing λ_k(x), but the error formula itself is missing, making the argument harder to follow. This appears to be an OCR error where the formula wasn't properly extracted.

comments.title: Insufficient explanation for increasing eigenvalues with decreasing noise
comments.quote: We also find that the number of non-zero eigenvalues _increases_ as the noise level decreases, leading to a suboptimal PSNR slope that is less than 1 _._ 0.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that as noise decreases, the DNN denoiser deviates more from the optimal solution (which should have exactly 5 non-zero eigenvalues for a 5D manifold). This counterintuitive behavior - where the network becomes less optimal with less noise - is not adequately explained. One would expect that with less noise, it would be easier to identify the correct tangent space. The authors attribute this to 'the presence of curvature' but don't explain why curvature effects would worsen with decreasing noise. This appears to be a key empirical observation that supports their main claim about GAHB bias, but the mechanism is unclear.

comments.title: Underspecified disk dataset parameters
comments.quote: We consider a dataset of disk images with varying positions, sizes, and foreground/background intensities.
comments.explanation: The description of the disk manifold dataset lacks crucial details for reproducibility. The ranges and distributions for positions, sizes, and intensities are not specified. For example: What is the range of disk radii? Are positions uniformly distributed across the image? What are the intensity ranges? These parameters affect the manifold's curvature and could influence the observed behavior. Without these details, readers cannot assess whether the results might be artifacts of particular parameter choices.

comments.title: Vague relationship between manifold curvature and suboptimality
comments.quote: These results reveal that the inductive biases of the DNN are not perfectly aligned with low-dimensional manifolds, and that in the presence of the curvature, this suboptimality increases as the noise level decreases.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that suboptimality increases with curvature and decreasing noise, but don't quantify or explain this relationship. How is curvature measured for this manifold? Is it constant across different disk configurations? Why would curvature effects become more pronounced at lower noise levels? This seems to be a key mechanistic claim supporting their GAHB bias hypothesis, but it's stated without sufficient justification or explanation.

comments.title: Missing justification for optimal eigenvalue structure
comments.quote: Thus, the optimal Jacobian should have only five non-negligible eigenvalues, whose corresponding eigenvectors span the tangent space. The remaining eigenvectors should have shrinkage factors of _λ_ = 0, but are otherwise unconstrained.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the optimal solution should have exactly 5 non-zero eigenvalues corresponding to the tangent space dimensions, with all others having λ = 0. While this is intuitive for projection onto a subspace, it's not immediately obvious why the remaining eigenvectors are completely unconstrained rather than being driven to λ = 0 through the optimization. A brief explanation of why this structure is optimal would help readers understand the baseline against which the DNN's behavior is being compared.

comments.title: Incorrect distinction between piecewise linear and piecewise affine
comments.quote: If _f_ ( _y_ ) is computed with a DNN denoiser with no additive "bias" parameters, its input-output mapping is piecewise linear, as opposed to piecewise affine (Mohan* et al., 2020; Romano et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The authors make an incorrect distinction between 'piecewise linear' and 'piecewise affine'. A function is piecewise linear if it's linear on each piece of its domain, while a function is piecewise affine if it's affine (linear plus a constant) on each piece. A piecewise linear function is actually a special case of a piecewise affine function where the constant term is zero. The authors likely mean that bias-free networks are homogeneous of degree 1 (i.e., f(ax) = af(x)), which is different from the standard definition of piecewise linear. This could confuse readers familiar with these mathematical terms.

comments.title: Typo in UNet architecture description
comments.quote: UNet networks contain 3 decoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks (Ronneberger et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo in the UNet architecture description. It states '3 decoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks' but should likely say '3 encoder blocks, one mid-level block, and 3 decoder blocks' to match the standard UNet architecture described in Ronneberger et al. (2015). The subsequent description mentions encoder blocks with downsampling and decoder blocks with upsampling, confirming this interpretation.

comments.title: Undefined variable in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: _**x** t ←_ _**x** t−_ 1 + _h_ _**d** t_ + _γtzt ▷_ Perform a partial denoiser step and add noise
comments.explanation: In step 9 of Algorithm 1, the update uses 'dt' which is not defined anywhere in the algorithm. Step 5 computes 'st ← f(xt-1) - xt-1' which is described as 'Compute the score from the denoiser residual'. The algorithm then uses an undefined 'dt' in the update equation instead of 'st'. This appears to be either a typographical error (where 'dt' should be 'st') or an oversight where 'dt' should be defined. Without clarification, the algorithm is ambiguous and would be difficult to reproduce correctly. Given that step 5 computes the score and step 9 mentions 'perform a partial denoiser step', it's most likely that 'dt' should be 'st', but this should be explicitly corrected or clarified.

comments.explanation: The receptive field sizes in Table 1 show a pattern of approximately halving as resolution doubles (8×18 → 4×44 → 2×92), but the last entry '88×188' breaks this pattern dramatically. Given the context and the progression of the other values, this appears to be an OCR error. The most plausible intended value would be something like '1×188' or similar to maintain the pattern. Since this is likely an OCR artifact rather than an author error, and the paper's claims don't depend on this specific value, I'm flagging this only for completeness.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for non-proportional data requirements
comments.quote: However, the number of training images does not increase proportionally with the image size: the increase in needed to hit the threshold ∆ _N_ from 80 _×_ 80 to 160 _×_ 160 images is much smaller than the ∆ _N_ from 40 _×_ 40 to 80 _×_ 80. This observation is consistent with previous reports indicating that conditioned on coarser content of the image, learning the finer details requires less data due to the conditional Markov property of images (Kadkhodaie et al., 2023).
comments.explanation: The authors make an interesting observation about the non-proportional increase in required training data as image resolution increases, attributing it to the conditional Markov property of images. While this is plausible and supported by the cited reference, the explanation is quite brief. The conditional Markov property is mentioned without any formal definition or explanation of how it specifically leads to this observed pattern. Given that this is an empirical observation that could have important implications for scaling laws, a more detailed explanation or even a brief mathematical illustration would strengthen the claim.

comments.title: Underspecified noise level sampling for PSNR gap calculation
comments.quote: Generalization is measured as the difference between train and test PSNRs averaged across noise levels _σ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1].
comments.explanation: The method for calculating the PSNR gap mentions averaging across noise levels σ ∈ [0,1], but doesn't specify how these noise levels are sampled. Are they uniformly sampled? Logarithmically spaced? How many samples? This level of detail could affect the reported values and would be important for reproducibility. While this might be specified elsewhere in the paper, it's a key methodological detail that ideally should be included here.

comments.title: Questionable claim about optimal denoising performance
comments.quote: By construction, the optimal denoiser on shuffled faces has the same performance as the optimal denoiser on ordinary faces (unshuffling the image pixels, optimally denoising the face image, and then shuffling the pixels back).
comments.explanation: The claim that optimal performance is the same for shuffled and unshuffled faces is only true if the permutation is known to the denoiser. However, the experimental setup suggests the DNN denoiser must learn to handle the shuffled pixels without explicit knowledge of the permutation. When the permutation is unknown, the denoising problem becomes fundamentally harder - the denoiser cannot exploit spatial correlations and locality that are crucial for effective denoising. The authors' statement is misleading because it compares against an oracle that knows the permutation, while their DNN denoiser does not have access to this information.

comments.title: Ambiguity about permutation knowledge
comments.quote: The permutation was chosen randomly, and does not preserve locality, as neighboring pixels are mapped to independent positions.
comments.explanation: The passage does not clarify whether the permutation is: (1) fixed across all images in the dataset, (2) different for each image, or (3) known to the network during training. This is crucial for interpreting the results. If the permutation is fixed and known during training, the network could potentially learn to reverse it. If it varies per image or is unknown, the task becomes much harder. The experimental setup and interpretation of results depend critically on this detail.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a conceptually compelling and technically ambitious investigation into the inductive biases underlying generalization in diffusion-based generative models, with a strong emphasis on linking architectural priors to geometric structure in image data. The writing is generally clear and the narrative logically progresses from empirical observations of generalization to a deeper theoretical analysis of learned representations. The central claim—that generalization in diffusion models arises from an inductive bias toward geometry-adaptive harmonic bases—is intriguing and potentially significant, particularly given the growing importance of diffusion models in high-dimensional generative modeling. However, several high-level concerns limit the paper’s current impact and credibility. First, while the empirical observation that denoisers trained on non-overlapping datasets converge to similar functions is suggestive of strong generalization, the evidence is under-supported: the paper lacks quantitative metrics (e.g., functional similarity measures, sample diversity scores, or density estimation errors) to substantiate this claim rigorously. Second, the core notion of “geometry-adaptive harmonic representations” is introduced intuitively but not formalized—critical details about how these bases are identified, extracted, or estimated from trained networks are missing, undermining the reproducibility and technical grounding of the analysis. Third, the discussion of inductive biases as favoring GAHBs—even in suboptimal regimes—relies on qualitative interpretations without controlling for alternative explanations (e.g., architectural invariances or optimization dynamics). The use of bandlets as a theoretical anchor is promising but not fully exploited: the connection to known optimality results is mentioned but not leveraged to derive testable predictions or performance bounds. Finally, the paper would benefit from clearer delineation between hypothesis, empirical finding, and speculation—particularly in the discussion of low-dimensional manifold experiments, where the presence of “suboptimal components” attributed to GAHB bias is asserted without rigorous decomposition or ablation. Strengthening the empirical methodology, clarifying the mathematical framework, and providing more robust evidence for the causal role of GAHBs would substantially elevate the paper’s contribution to the foundational understanding of generalization in modern generative models.

comments.title: Missing equation for marginalization of noisy image density
comments.quote:
The density _pσ_ ( _y_ ) of noisy images is then related to _p_ ( _x_ ) through marginalization over _x_ :

where _gσ_ ( _z_ ) is the density of _z_ .
comments.explanation: The text states that the density _pσ_(_y_) is obtained by marginalizing over _x_, and mentions that _gσ_(_z_) is the density of the noise _z_, but the actual mathematical expression for this marginalization is missing (there is a line break or blank space where an equation should be). Given the context—specifically, that _y = x + z_ with _z ∼ N(0, σ² Id)_—the correct expression should be something like _pσ(y) = ∫ p(x) gσ(y − x) dx_, i.e., the convolution of _p_ with the Gaussian. This is a standard result in diffusion models and score-based methods. The surrounding text correctly describes that _pσ(y)_ is a convolution of _p(x)_ with a Gaussian, so the prose is accurate. However, the absence of the actual formula in a section explicitly labeled '2.1 DIFFUSION MODELS AND DENOISING', where mathematical rigor is expected, constitutes an error in presentation. Since this is a key definitional step and the equation is trivially reconstructable by experts, it may be considered an omission due to layout or OCR. But given the instruction to flag only issues that would remain problematic under the most plausible symbol correction, and since no plausible OCR error can explain a completely missing equation line, this appears to be a substantive omission. A reader might proceed unimpeded, but the formal definition is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'subspace spanned by GAHB vectors' in manifold denoising context
comments.quote: We observe that the DNN denoiser closely approximates this projection, but also partially retains content lying within a subspace spanned by a set of additional GAHB vectors.
comments.explanation: The claim here is that when trained on low-dimensional manifolds, DNN denoisers project noisy images close to the tangent space (optimal), but additionally retain components in a subspace spanned by GAHB vectors—interpreted as a signature of inductive bias. However, the term 'GAHB vectors' is used prior in the context of image geometry (e.g., harmonic functions adapted to contours), while here the data lies on abstract low-dimensional manifolds possibly unrelated to visual geometry. It's unclear what 'GAHB vectors' means in this new context—can such harmonic bases be defined intrinsically on arbitrary manifolds? Or are they referring to spatial GAHB-like features learned in pixel space, regardless of the manifold structure? The sentence implies that GAHB is a fixed class of functions in pixel space (since networks operate in pixel domain), but the phrase 'additional GAHB vectors' suggests they supplement the tangent basis with extra harmonic modes in image space. While this interpretation aligns with the inductive bias idea—that networks favor spatially oscillatory, geometrically adapted representations even when suboptimal—it could mislead a reader into thinking GAHB is being defined intrinsically on the data manifold. The ambiguity lies in whether 'GAHB vectors' refers to functions in the ambient pixel space (plausible) or on the manifold (questionable). Without clarification, a careful reader might incorrectly infer that GAHB is a universal functional basis adaptable to any manifold, which is not necessarily supported. The core idea—that the network fails to fully restrict to the tangent space and instead leaks energy into structured, harmonic-like directions—is sound and consistent with earlier claims, but the phrasing risks overgeneralizing the scope of GAHB.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Denoiser's Blindness to Noise Level
comments.quote: These denoisers are universal and blind: they operate on all noise levels without having noise level as an input Mohan* et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: At first, I was concerned that the claim that the denoiser is blind to the noise level σ contradicts standard practice in diffusion models, where conditioning on σ is typical to handle varying noise levels. The paper states that their denoisers are trained without explicit noise level input (i.e., blind), relying on Mohan et al. (2020) for justification. However, in most modern diffusion models—including score-based models—the score function s_θ(y) is explicitly dependent on σ, and denoisers are usually conditioned on σ to produce accurate estimates across scales. The authors likely mean that their architecture does not take σ as an explicit input *during training*, but this raises questions: How can a single network effectively denoise across all σ without adaptation? Do they implicitly assume that training data includes samples at many noise levels, allowing the network to learn an average behavior? This is plausible if the training set contains many noisy versions of clean images across a range of σ, but the method section doesn't clarify this crucial detail. Without specifying how noise levels are sampled during training or whether the network sees mixed-noise minibatches, it's unclear how 'blind' denoising generalizes across scales. Given that performance in Figure 1 is shown across input PSNR (hence varying σ), the model must somehow encode multi-scale behavior. While 'blind' denoising exists (e.g., in some BM3D variants), in deep learning this is non-trivial and requires careful design (e.g., noise-embedding layers or multi-noise training). The explanation being 'universal and blind' is underspecified and leaves a motivated reader uncertain about reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Score Estimation via MSE Minimization
comments.quote:
The score is learned by training a denoiser _fθ_ ( _y_ ) to minimize the mean squared error (MSE) (Raphan & Simoncelli, 2011; Vincent, 2011):

so that _fθ_ ( _y_ ) _≈_ E _x_ [ _x | y_ ]. This estimated conditional mean is used to recover the estimated score using eq. (3): _sθ_ ( _y_ ) = ( _fθ_ ( _y_ ) _− y_ ) _/σ_[2]
comments.explanation: Initially, the flow seemed standard: train a denoiser by minimizing MSE to estimate E[x|y], then use that to approximate the score. This is consistent with known connections between denoising and score estimation (e.g., the Tweedie formula). However, I recalled that minimization of MSE=E[||x−f_θ(y)||²] leads to f_θ(y) → E[x|y], which is correct. But the score ∇_y log p_σ(y) is related to the *residual* (E[x|y] − y)/σ² — this is a well-known identity from the Stein's lemma and the score-matching literature. So far, so good. The issue arises in the missing equation label (3) and the abrupt jump: the text says 'using eq. (3)', but no equation (3) appears in the passage. This might be due to OCR or boundary truncation. Nevertheless, even assuming eq. (3) defines s_θ(y) = (f_θ(y) − y)/σ², there's a subtle but important point: this formula holds *exactly* for the optimal denoiser f_⋆(y)=E[x|y], but is only an approximation when f_θ is suboptimal. The paper treats it as a direct plug-in estimate, which is standard, but should clarify that any error in f_θ propagates nonlinearly into s_θ due to division by σ², especially at low noise. This becomes crucial when integrating over all σ. While the overall derivation is justified in the literature (and cited), the omission of the equation and the lack of cautionary note about amplification of errors at small σ could mislead a reader about stability. Given the paper's focus on integrated error across noise levels, this deserves mention. Still, since this is a well-established result (and Appendix D.2 is referenced), the issue is mainly presentational rather than technical.

comments.title: Ambiguous Phrasing of Model Variance and Generalization
comments.quote: Here, we define generalization as near-zero model variance (i.e., an absence of overfitting), which is agnostic to model bias.
comments.explanation: At first read, this seemed like a reasonable operational definition: if two models trained on different data converge to the same function, variance is low and generalization is strong. That matches the empirical approach in Figure 2. However, in standard statistical learning theory, 'generalization' typically refers to small gap between training and test error, which combines both bias and variance. The authors are redefining 'generalization' narrowly as low variance, explicitly decoupling it from bias. While this is acceptable if clearly stated, the risk is that readers may interpret 'generalization' in the conventional sense and conclude the model is accurate (low bias), which is not implied. The paper later evaluates model bias separately on synthetic data, so the separation is justified. But the phrasing 'this is the subject of Section 2.2' might mislead: Section 2.2 shows convergence of models on held-out data and agreement between independently trained ones, which supports low variance, but it doesn't prove the converged function is close to optimal (i.e., low bias). The claim 'generalization implies diffusion-generated samples are distinct from training data' (Abstract) rests on this variance-bias decomposition. While logically sound in context, the term 'generalization' is used in a non-standard, narrower way. A more precise phrasing might be 'strong convergence' or 'low model variance', reserving 'generalization' for test performance. This could confuse careful readers familiar with learning theory.

comments.title: Potential OCR Artifact in Test/Train PSNR Labeling
comments.quote: 4845 N: 100000identity
comments.explanation: In the picture text under Figure 1, the line '4845 N: 100000identity' stands out as suspect. Given the pattern above, each line starts with a number (declining from 63 to 48) and then 'N: X'. But '4845' likely results from a line break or OCR misread: '48' from the previous line continuation and '45' from the next, or '48' and then '45 N:...' misaligned. Also, 'identity' appears out of place—possibly a corrupted version of 'Input PSNR' or a label fragment. The original figure probably has two plots: one showing training error vs input PSNR (left) and test error (right), with curves labeled by N. The value near 48 likely corresponds to output PSNR at high input PSNR for N=100000, consistent with ceiling effects. But the concatenation '4845' and the word 'identity' (perhaps meant to be a legend entry like 'Train' or 'Test') suggest OCR failure. Since the surrounding text explains that train and test PSNR become identical at N=1e5, and that performance plateaus, the intent is clear. However, if uncorrected, a reader might misparse the data points or think 'identity' refers to some condition (e.g., identity mapping). While not a flaw in the authors’ work, it’s a critical OCR issue affecting figure interpretation. Since the instruction says to flag only if the issue persists under plausible correction, and '48' and '45' likely belong to separate tick marks, this is an OCR flaw that should be corrected in final typesetting.

comments.title: Incorrect Shrinkage Factor for Oracle Denoiser
comments.quote: _⟨x,ek⟩_[2] which is minimized when _λk_ ( _x_ ) = _⟨x,ek⟩_[2] + _σ_[2] _[.]_[ The coefficient] _[ λ][k]_[(] _[x]_[)][ thus acts as a soft threshold:_
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expression for the optimal shrinkage factor λk(x) appears incorrect: it claims that λk(x) = ⟨x,ek⟩² + σ², which is dimensionally inconsistent (this would make λk have units of squared amplitude, not a dimensionless scaling factor). Given that the denoiser output is expressed as a linear combination involving λk(y), λk should represent a multiplicative factor between 0 and 1. The correct expression for the oracle Wiener filter in a basis is λk(x) = ⟨x, ek⟩² / (⟨x, ek⟩² + σ²), which *does* behave like a soft threshold—close to 1 when signal dominates, close to 0 otherwise. This is standard in denoising theory (e.g., Donoho & Johnstone, Mallat). The quoted text instead gives the denominator of the Wiener filter, not the shrinkage coefficient itself. While the surrounding discussion mentions soft thresholding and correct asymptotics, the formula is clearly wrong. Appendix D.4 is cited, but even assuming correctness there, this passage misstates the result. The OCR does not appear to be the issue here—'[2]' consistently denotes squaring elsewhere, and 'σ[2]' means σ². Thus, this is not a transcription error but a material error in the formulation of a key theoretical quantity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Adaptive Basis Definition Due to OCR Artifacts
comments.quote: In most cases, the optimal adaptive basis ( _e[⋆] k_[(] _[y]_[))] 1 _≤k≤d_[is not known.][Rather than aiming for exact]
comments.explanation: The text appears to be corrupted by OCR errors, making it difficult to parse. The notation 'e[⋆] k[(] [y]_[)]' likely intends to represent e*_k(y), the k-th eigenvector of the optimal denoiser’s Jacobian at y. The surrounding context defines e_k(y) as Jacobian eigenvectors, and f*(y) as the optimal denoiser, so e*_k(y) would be a natural notation for the optimal basis. The splicing '[is not known.][Rather than aiming for exact]' suggests a line break or scanning artifact. Although the intended meaning—'the optimal adaptive basis (e*_k(y))_{1≤k≤d} is not known'—is recoverable given the context, the current rendering is grammatically broken and potentially misleading, especially since 'e[⋆] k' could be misread as e*_k or e_k^*. In an academic paper, such notation should be typeset clearly. However, given that this is noted as OCR-extracted and the full meaning can be reconstructed, it does not constitute a fatal flaw. Still, for clarity and rigor, the notation should be corrected to unambiguously represent e^*_k(y). As it stands, a careful reader might question whether ⋆ modifies e_k or is part of a misread symbol.

comments.title: Misleading Interpretation of SURE in Low-Rank Approximation
comments.quote: To minimize MSE, the denoiser must trade off the approximate “rank” of the Jacobian (the trace is the sum of the eigenvalues) against an estimate of the denoising error: _∥y − f_ ( _y_ ) _∥_[2] _− σ_[2] _d_ .
comments.explanation: The quote attempts to connect SURE (Stein’s unbiased risk estimate) to a trade-off between the trace of the Jacobian (which relates to degrees of freedom) and the residual ||y − f(y)||². However, SURE states that an unbiased estimate of the MSE is ||y − f(y)||² + 2σ² div f(y) − σ² d, where div f(y) = Tr(∇f(y)). The expression in the paper, ||y − f(y)||² − σ² d, is *not* part of the SURE trade-off—rather, the correct trade-off is between ||y − f(y)||² and Tr(∇f(y)). The subtraction of σ² d is constant and does not participate in the optimization; it's part of the bias correction. By writing the trade-off as being against '||y − f(y)||² − σ² d', the paper misrepresents the structure of SURE. While the intent—to highlight a trade-off between residual fit and Jacobian 'complexity'—is correct and well-motivated, the formulation incorrectly identifies which terms are being balanced. This could mislead a reader unfamiliar with SURE into thinking the risk estimate depends on a shifted residual rather than understanding that divergence (trace of Jacobian) is the regularizing term. This is a subtle but important misstatement of a foundational result.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Oracle Denoising Error Expression
comments.quote:
Appendix D.4 then shows that the oracle denoising error is the expected value of

where _xM_ =[] _⟨x,ek⟩_[2] _>σ_[2] _[⟨][x, e][k][⟩][e][k]_[is the] _[ M]_[-term approximation of] _[ x]_[ with the] _[ M]_[basis coefficients] _⟨x, ek⟩_ above the noise level, and _∼_ means that the two terms are of the same order up to multiplicative constants (here smaller than 2).
comments.explanation: The passage states that the oracle denoising error is the expected value of some expression, but that expression is missing—there is a line break or OCR error where a mathematical term should appear (e.g., ||x − x_M||² + M σ²). From context and standard theory, we can infer it refers to the expected oracle risk 𝔼[||x − x̂||²] ∼ 𝔼[||x − x_M||² + M σ²], which is standard in oracle inequalities. While the general intuition is preserved—sparsity leads to better denoising—the absence of the actual expression disrupts the logical flow and prevents verification of the claim. Given that the paper presents this as a key step in establishing asymptotic performance, and that Appendix D.4 is cited, the omission here (likely due to OCR) renders the argument incomplete *in this section*. However, since this is attributed to an appendix and the concept is well-known in the field (e.g., Donoho), this is more of a presentation flaw than a technical one.
